name: Semiconductor Infringement
description: Infringement scoring overrides for Semiconductor patents. Emphasizes hardware necessary-implication and chip architecture analysis.
scoringGuidance: Semiconductor patents describe circuit-level implementations that are rarely visible in product documentation. Heavily weight necessary implication.
If a product uses a specific chip or SoC, published datasheets and block diagrams provide strong evidence of internal architecture.
Process-level patents (FinFET, gate-all-around, etc.) apply to any product fabricated on the relevant process node.
Memory and interconnect patents apply broadly — any modern SoC necessarily includes cache hierarchies, bus protocols, and memory controllers.
necessaryImplicationGuidance: Any modern SoC necessarily includes: (1) clock distribution network, (2) power management unit, (3) bus interconnect fabric, (4) cache hierarchy, (5) memory controller. Any wireless SoC necessarily includes: (1) ADC/DAC, (2) PLL/synthesizer, (3) PA driver, (4) baseband processor. Product datasheets and block diagrams are strong evidence of internal architecture even when full schematics are unavailable.

questionOverrides.reasoningPrompt: Hardware patents heavily depend on necessary implication. Identify which undocumented circuit blocks must exist based on the product's described functionality and any referenced chips/SoCs.
Semiconductor product docs are often sparse. Datasheets, block diagrams, and reference designs provide strong evidence. Note what documentation types are available.
